behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at a module handoff (classifier/request-builder -> API client payload). Unit->Integration proof: an interface-contract bug can occur when wiring modules (e.g., taxonomy list dropped during request assembly). REAL-INFRA: not required; this can be caught with in-process wiring and a capturing fake client, so reject System. REJECTION-GATE (higher levels): behavior text only requires request content ('includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels'), not real network behavior, model quality, or multi-step agent flow.
behaviors.test_description: Wire classifier with real request-builder + API client adapter interface and a spy/fake transport that records the final outgoing payload object/string; assert every configured taxonomy label is present on each call.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is local mapping/validation logic on returned label, not a cross-module failure boundary. No boundary proof for Unit->Integration is needed because the behavior can fail entirely inside one function/object. REJECTION-GATE (higher levels): behavior text is pure post-processing ('returns "unknown"') and does not require real OS/network/model output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Call the label-normalization/classify path with a stubbed API result containing an out-of-taxonomy label and assert returned value is exactly "unknown".

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration proof: behavior crosses module boundary to external classifier call. Integration->System proof: repeated real external calls are required; in-process stubs cannot expose live variability. System->Agentic proof: correctness depends on real model output stability/non-determinism across runs for identical input. REJECTION-GATE (Workflow): behavior is repeated single-step classification, not a sequence of multiple agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Run N repeated classifications (same document, same settings) against the real AI API in a controlled window; assert label consistency rate meets requirement (e.g., 100% or agreed threshold) and capture variance if it fails.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration proof: budget enforcement depends on handoff between tokenizer/budget logic and API request construction. Integration->System proof: real provider token accounting (actual consumed tokens) is external behavior; mocks can hide tokenizer/accounting mismatches. REJECTION-GATE (Agentic/Workflow): behavior concerns token usage, not model output quality or multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Execute real classification calls for representative 'typical' documents and assert provider-reported token usage (or authoritative usage metadata) is <= configured per-call budget.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at module interaction (API error handling -> retry/backoff policy -> caller result). Unit->Integration proof: correctness depends on wiring retry logic with API client responses and delay mechanism. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) real OS/network/subprocess not required; deterministic 429-like stub response and fake/controlled clock can reproduce failure, so reject System. (b) no AI output-quality dependence, so not Agentic. (c) no multi-agent chain, so not Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Use a stub API client that returns rate-limit signal first, then success; run classifier with controlled clock/sleeper and assert it waits/backs off, retries, and ultimately returns success instead of surfacing the first error.
